title: B12 Deficiency
oneLiner: Macrocytic anaemia and/or neuropathy due to low B12; common in elderly, vegans, post-gastrectomy, and pernicious anaemia; treat promptly to prevent irreversible cord damage.

sections.Vitamin B12 Deficiency: **One-liner:** Macrocytic anaemia and/or neuropathy due to low B12; common in elderly, vegans, post-gastrectomy, and pernicious anaemia; treat promptly to prevent irreversible cord damage.
sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute severe anaemia (Hb < 60 g/L) with heart failure**  
  - Single finding: *Bounding pulse, raised JVP, and dyspnoea at rest despite no bleeding* – this is high-output failure from severe anaemia. Give blood urgently (not just B12).
- **Subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord (SACD)**  
  - Single finding: *Loss of vibration sense + proprioception + spastic paraparesis + extensor plantars* – this is a neurological emergency; delay in B12 replacement may result in irreversible paralysis. Start IM hydroxocobalamin immediately.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Progressive fatigue, pallor, dyspnoea on exertion (anaemia)
  - Paraesthesia (tingling in hands/feet), unsteadiness, gait ataxia (neuropathy)
  - Cognitive changes: poor memory, confusion, depression
  - Glossitis (smooth, red, sore tongue)
  - Jaundice (mild, due to ineffective erythropoiesis)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Pallor, glossitis, mild jaundice
  - Neurological: impaired vibration sense (>1st toe is early sign), proprioceptive loss, Romberg positive, spasticity, Babinski upgoing
  - If profound: orthostatic hypotension (dorsal column involvement)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Most likely -> less likely, with 1-line differentiators:**
  - **Folate deficiency:** also macrocytic, but neurological signs absent (B12 does not cause folate deficiency). Check red cell folate.
  - **Myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS):** macrocytic anaemia in elderly; B12 and folate normal; blood film may show dysplastic cells; needs bone marrow biopsy.
  - **Alcohol excess / liver disease:** macrocytosis without anaemia or neuropathy; GGT raised; B12 normal.
  - **Hypothyroidism:** can cause macrocytic anaemia and fatigue; TSH high, B12 normal.
  - **Drug-induced macrocytosis:** methotrexate, azathioprine, antiretrovirals; check drug history.
- **Splitter:** *Presence of neurological symptoms (especially vibration/proprioception loss) – unlikely to be folate deficiency; always check B12 first.*
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:**
  - Full blood count + blood film (macrocytosis, hypersegmented neutrophils)
  - ECG if chest pain or signs of heart failure (look for ischaemia; not specific)
- **Bloods:**
  - Serum B12 (vitamin B12) – low < 150 pmol/L (though levels 150-300 can be borderline)
  - Red cell folate (not serum folate) – if low, consider combined deficiency
  - Anti-intrinsic factor antibodies (positive in pernicious anaemia – specific)
  - Anti-parietal cell antibodies (less specific)
  - If pernicious anaemia suspected: check gastrin (elevated) and pepsinogen (low)
  - LDH, haptoglobin (elevated LDH + low haptoglobin suggests haemolysis, common in B12 deficiency)
  - Bilirubin (mildly elevated due to ineffective erythropoiesis)
- **Imaging:**
  - Not routine. Consider MRI spine (cervical/thoracic) if neurological signs to rule out cord compression or assess SACD.
- **Special tests:**
  - Schilling test (now rarely used; replaced by antibody testing + response to therapy)
  - Upper GI endoscopy with biopsies if pernicious anaemia suspected (gastric atrophy)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - No single score; diagnosis based on low B12 ± macrocytic anaemia ± neurological signs ± positive antibodies. A therapeutic response to IM B12 confirms deficiency.
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Advice:** *Folate level (red cell folate) must be checked before B12 replacement.* Giving folic acid alone without B12 in combined deficiency can precipitate or worsen neurological damage. If you start B12, you must also check folate; if low, treat both.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If severe anaemia (Hb < 70 or symptomatic) → arrange urgent transfusion (give 1-2 units with caution; can precipitate volume overload). Start B12 simultaneously.
  - If neurological signs (SACD) → give first dose of hydroxocobalamin 1mg IM **stat** while waiting for confirmatory results.
  - Ensure folate level sent.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue B12 replacement according to BNF schedule (see Drugs & Doses)
  - If anaemic: monitor Hb, reticulocyte count (should rise in 5-7 days). Expect improvement in fatigue over 1-2 weeks.
  - If neurological: physiotherapy referral, monitor gait and sensation daily.
  - Check for other causes: alcohol, vegan diet, GI surgery, proton pump inhibitor use.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Pernicious anaemia:** lifelong hydroxocobalamin every 3 months
  - **Dietary deficiency or vegan:** consider oral B12 50-100mcg daily (if absorption intact) or continue IM if malabsorption suspected
  - **Post-gastrectomy/bariatric:** lifelong IM B12 every 3 months
  - **Neurological residual:** may need more intensive schedule initially (every 1-2 weeks until improvement stops), then switch to 2-monthly.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** oral fluids as tolerated; IV if needed only for dehydration.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** **YES** – low molecular weight heparin (enoxaparin) once daily (standard dose per weight/renal function) unless contraindicated (thrombocytopenia/bleeding risk).
  - **Diet/NBM:** normal diet, encourage high folate/B12 foods (but not therapeutic)
  - **Observation frequency:** NEWS chart. If anaemia significant (Hb < 90), consider minimum 4-hourly observations.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agent — Hydroxocobalamin (IM)**
  - **Dose:** 1mg IM three times a week for 2 weeks (6 doses) for pernicious anaemia / deficiency without neurological involvement
  - **Neurological involvement:** 1mg IM on alternate days until no further improvement (usually 1-2 weeks), then 1mg every 2 months
  - **Maintenance:** 1mg IM every 3 months lifelong (for pernicious anaemia)
  - **Route:** deep intramuscular (gluteal or lateral thigh) – never IV
- **Common alternative:** Cyanocobalamin (oral) – only for dietary deficiency with normal absorption (e.g., vegan). Dose: 50-100mcg daily (or 1-2mg daily in some guidelines). Check local policy.
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - None absolute. Avoid intramuscular if coagulopathy (use subcutaneous if needed, but efficacy not proven; consider IV route with caution – check local policy). No significant drug interactions.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - No dose adjustment needed for renal or hepatic impairment. Dose is therapeutic, not dependent on clearance.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Giving oral folic acid without B12** – can precipitate acute neurological deterioration. Always check and treat B12 first or simultaneously.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - **Transfusion-associated circulatory overload (TACO)** – in elderly with severe anaemia, transfuse slowly (e.g., 1 unit over 4 hours) and consider diuretic.
  - **Hypokalaemia** – massive reutilization of iron and folate during erythropoiesis can cause rapid fall in K+. Monitor K+ daily during first week of therapy.
  - **Allergic reaction to hydroxocobalamin** – rare (urticaria, anaphylaxis)
- **Chronic:**
  - **Irreversible neurological damage** if treatment started late (especially SACD)
  - **Increased risk of gastric cancer** – pernicious anaemia carries a 2-3 fold increased risk; consider surveillance endoscopy in older patients or those with dysplasia.
  - **Osteoporosis** – pernicious anaemia associated with reduced BMD (check calcium/vitamin D)
sections.Prognosis:
- **Mortality:** Very low if treated early. Anaemia fully reversible within 2-4 weeks.
- **Neurological recovery:** Good if treated within days to weeks of symptom onset. If symptoms >6 months, recovery may be incomplete. Paresthesias often resolve; ataxia may persist.
- **Chronicity:** Pernicious anaemia requires lifelong B12; otherwise normal life expectancy.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always check red cell folate before starting B12** – prevents missing combined deficiency and avoids precipitating cord degeneration.
- **In patients with macrocytic anaemia, ask about diet and previous GI surgery** – many cases are missed because clinicians don't take a good dietary history.
- **Monitor reticulocyte count after 5 days** – a rise by >3% confirms B12 effect. No rise suggests another diagnosis (MDS, alcohol).
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not give folic acid alone** – unwittingly worsens B12 deficiency neurology.
- **Do not delay B12 therapy while awaiting antibody results** – if clinical suspicion high (esp. with neuropathy), start IM B12 immediately. Antibodies take days.
- **Do not rely solely on serum B12** – borderline levels (150-300 pmol/L) can still be deficient in presence of symptoms; consider methylmalonic acid (MMA) or homocysteine if available. Not routinely in ED, but ask lab if clinical doubt.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** 
  - Worsening neurological symptoms (ataxia, falls, weakness) – may need higher dose frequency.
  - Severe headache, breathlessness, or palpitations – suggests worsening anaemia.
  - After transfusion: return if signs of fluid overload (leg swelling, breathlessness, weight gain).
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP in 2 weeks for check of Hb and reticulocytes. 
  - If pernicious anaemia: lifelong specialist follow-up (haematology or gastroenterology). Ensure GP aware of 3-monthly B12 injection schedule.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **When to call senior/ICU:**
  - **NEWS2 ≥ 5** with signs of heart failure or respiratory distress (especially after transfusion)
  - **Lactate > 2.5 mmol/L** (may indicate ischaemia from anaemia)
  - **GCS < 15** or new confusion (consider severe B12 encephalopathy – rare)
  - **Haemoglobin < 60 g/L** with cardiovascular compromise (call reg/haematology for urgent transfusion)
  - **Anaphylaxis** after B12 injection (ABC support, call crash team)

rawMarkdown:
## Vitamin B12 Deficiency
**One-liner:** Macrocytic anaemia and/or neuropathy due to low B12; common in elderly, vegans, post-gastrectomy, and pernicious anaemia; treat promptly to prevent irreversible cord damage.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute severe anaemia (Hb < 60 g/L) with heart failure**  
  - Single finding: *Bounding pulse, raised JVP, and dyspnoea at rest despite no bleeding* – this is high-output failure from severe anaemia. Give blood urgently (not just B12).
- **Subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord (SACD)**  
  - Single finding: *Loss of vibration sense + proprioception + spastic paraparesis + extensor plantars* – this is a neurological emergency; delay in B12 replacement may result in irreversible paralysis. Start IM hydroxocobalamin immediately.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:**
  - Progressive fatigue, pallor, dyspnoea on exertion (anaemia)
  - Paraesthesia (tingling in hands/feet), unsteadiness, gait ataxia (neuropathy)
  - Cognitive changes: poor memory, confusion, depression
  - Glossitis (smooth, red, sore tongue)
  - Jaundice (mild, due to ineffective erythropoiesis)
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Pallor, glossitis, mild jaundice
  - Neurological: impaired vibration sense (>1st toe is early sign), proprioceptive loss, Romberg positive, spasticity, Babinski upgoing
  - If profound: orthostatic hypotension (dorsal column involvement)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Most likely -> less likely, with 1-line differentiators:**
  - **Folate deficiency:** also macrocytic, but neurological signs absent (B12 does not cause folate deficiency). Check red cell folate.
  - **Myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS):** macrocytic anaemia in elderly; B12 and folate normal; blood film may show dysplastic cells; needs bone marrow biopsy.
  - **Alcohol excess / liver disease:** macrocytosis without anaemia or neuropathy; GGT raised; B12 normal.
  - **Hypothyroidism:** can cause macrocytic anaemia and fatigue; TSH high, B12 normal.
  - **Drug-induced macrocytosis:** methotrexate, azathioprine, antiretrovirals; check drug history.
- **Splitter:** *Presence of neurological symptoms (especially vibration/proprioception loss) – unlikely to be folate deficiency; always check B12 first.*

### Investigations
- **Bedside:**
  - Full blood count + blood film (macrocytosis, hypersegmented neutrophils)
  - ECG if chest pain or signs of heart failure (look for ischaemia; not specific)
- **Bloods:**
  - Serum B12 (vitamin B12) – low < 150 pmol/L (though levels 150-300 can be borderline)
  - Red cell folate (not serum folate) – if low, consider combined deficiency
  - Anti-intrinsic factor antibodies (positive in pernicious anaemia – specific)
  - Anti-parietal cell antibodies (less specific)
  - If pernicious anaemia suspected: check gastrin (elevated) and pepsinogen (low)
  - LDH, haptoglobin (elevated LDH + low haptoglobin suggests haemolysis, common in B12 deficiency)
  - Bilirubin (mildly elevated due to ineffective erythropoiesis)
- **Imaging:**
  - Not routine. Consider MRI spine (cervical/thoracic) if neurological signs to rule out cord compression or assess SACD.
- **Special tests:**
  - Schilling test (now rarely used; replaced by antibody testing + response to therapy)
  - Upper GI endoscopy with biopsies if pernicious anaemia suspected (gastric atrophy)
- **Diagnostic criteria / scoring system:**
  - No single score; diagnosis based on low B12 ± macrocytic anaemia ± neurological signs ± positive antibodies. A therapeutic response to IM B12 confirms deficiency.
- **Never-miss test:**
  - **Advice:** *Folate level (red cell folate) must be checked before B12 replacement.* Giving folic acid alone without B12 in combined deficiency can precipitate or worsen neurological damage. If you start B12, you must also check folate; if low, treat both.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - If severe anaemia (Hb < 70 or symptomatic) → arrange urgent transfusion (give 1-2 units with caution; can precipitate volume overload). Start B12 simultaneously.
  - If neurological signs (SACD) → give first dose of hydroxocobalamin 1mg IM **stat** while waiting for confirmatory results.
  - Ensure folate level sent.
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**
  - Continue B12 replacement according to BNF schedule (see Drugs & Doses)
  - If anaemic: monitor Hb, reticulocyte count (should rise in 5-7 days). Expect improvement in fatigue over 1-2 weeks.
  - If neurological: physiotherapy referral, monitor gait and sensation daily.
  - Check for other causes: alcohol, vegan diet, GI surgery, proton pump inhibitor use.
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - **Pernicious anaemia:** lifelong hydroxocobalamin every 3 months
  - **Dietary deficiency or vegan:** consider oral B12 50-100mcg daily (if absorption intact) or continue IM if malabsorption suspected
  - **Post-gastrectomy/bariatric:** lifelong IM B12 every 3 months
  - **Neurological residual:** may need more intensive schedule initially (every 1-2 weeks until improvement stops), then switch to 2-monthly.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
  - **Fluids:** oral fluids as tolerated; IV if needed only for dehydration.
  - **VTE prophylaxis:** **YES** – low molecular weight heparin (enoxaparin) once daily (standard dose per weight/renal function) unless contraindicated (thrombocytopenia/bleeding risk).
  - **Diet/NBM:** normal diet, encourage high folate/B12 foods (but not therapeutic)
  - **Observation frequency:** NEWS chart. If anaemia significant (Hb < 90), consider minimum 4-hourly observations.

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agent — Hydroxocobalamin (IM)**
  - **Dose:** 1mg IM three times a week for 2 weeks (6 doses) for pernicious anaemia / deficiency without neurological involvement
  - **Neurological involvement:** 1mg IM on alternate days until no further improvement (usually 1-2 weeks), then 1mg every 2 months
  - **Maintenance:** 1mg IM every 3 months lifelong (for pernicious anaemia)
  - **Route:** deep intramuscular (gluteal or lateral thigh) – never IV
- **Common alternative:** Cyanocobalamin (oral) – only for dietary deficiency with normal absorption (e.g., vegan). Dose: 50-100mcg daily (or 1-2mg daily in some guidelines). Check local policy.
- **Key contraindications/interactions:**
  - None absolute. Avoid intramuscular if coagulopathy (use subcutaneous if needed, but efficacy not proven; consider IV route with caution – check local policy). No significant drug interactions.
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**
  - No dose adjustment needed for renal or hepatic impairment. Dose is therapeutic, not dependent on clearance.
- **Most dangerous dose error:**
  - **Giving oral folic acid without B12** – can precipitate acute neurological deterioration. Always check and treat B12 first or simultaneously.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - **Transfusion-associated circulatory overload (TACO)** – in elderly with severe anaemia, transfuse slowly (e.g., 1 unit over 4 hours) and consider diuretic.
  - **Hypokalaemia** – massive reutilization of iron and folate during erythropoiesis can cause rapid fall in K+. Monitor K+ daily during first week of therapy.
  - **Allergic reaction to hydroxocobalamin** – rare (urticaria, anaphylaxis)
- **Chronic:**
  - **Irreversible neurological damage** if treatment started late (especially SACD)
  - **Increased risk of gastric cancer** – pernicious anaemia carries a 2-3 fold increased risk; consider surveillance endoscopy in older patients or those with dysplasia.
  - **Osteoporosis** – pernicious anaemia associated with reduced BMD (check calcium/vitamin D)

### Prognosis
- **Mortality:** Very low if treated early. Anaemia fully reversible within 2-4 weeks.
- **Neurological recovery:** Good if treated within days to weeks of symptom onset. If symptoms >6 months, recovery may be incomplete. Paresthesias often resolve; ataxia may persist.
- **Chronicity:** Pernicious anaemia requires lifelong B12; otherwise normal life expectancy.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always check red cell folate before starting B12** – prevents missing combined deficiency and avoids precipitating cord degeneration.
- **In patients with macrocytic anaemia, ask about diet and previous GI surgery** – many cases are missed because clinicians don't take a good dietary history.
- **Monitor reticulocyte count after 5 days** – a rise by >3% confirms B12 effect. No rise suggests another diagnosis (MDS, alcohol).

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not give folic acid alone** – unwittingly worsens B12 deficiency neurology.
- **Do not delay B12 therapy while awaiting antibody results** – if clinical suspicion high (esp. with neuropathy), start IM B12 immediately. Antibodies take days.
- **Do not rely solely on serum B12** – borderline levels (150-300 pmol/L) can still be deficient in presence of symptoms; consider methylmalonic acid (MMA) or homocysteine if available. Not routinely in ED, but ask lab if clinical doubt.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** 
  - Worsening neurological symptoms (ataxia, falls, weakness) – may need higher dose frequency.
  - Severe headache, breathlessness, or palpitations – suggests worsening anaemia.
  - After transfusion: return if signs of fluid overload (leg swelling, breathlessness, weight gain).
- **Follow-up:**
  - GP in 2 weeks for check of Hb and reticulocytes. 
  - If pernicious anaemia: lifelong specialist follow-up (haematology or gastroenterology). Ensure GP aware of 3-monthly B12 injection schedule.

### Escalation Criteria
- **When to call senior/ICU:**
  - **NEWS2 ≥ 5** with signs of heart failure or respiratory distress (especially after transfusion)
  - **Lactate > 2.5 mmol/L** (may indicate ischaemia from anaemia)
  - **GCS < 15** or new confusion (consider severe B12 encephalopathy – rare)
  - **Haemoglobin < 60 g/L** with cardiovascular compromise (call reg/haematology for urgent transfusion)
  - **Anaphylaxis** after B12 injection (ABC support, call crash team)
